title: Comparing Planners Head-to-Head
concept: Analyzing how ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, and Tree of Thoughts perform on the same puzzle.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what does an AI 'planner' help an agent do?

options.text: Break a big task into ordered steps toward a goal

exercises.explain: A planner is the part of an agent that figures out HOW to reach a goal by choosing steps and their order. That's why comparing different planners matters, they each choose steps differently.

cards.title: Three Planners, One Puzzle
cards.body: Give ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, and Tree of Thoughts the same riddle. Watch how differently each one thinks its way to the answer.

cards.title: What We're Comparing
cards.body: ReAct thinks then acts, step by step. Plan-and-Execute writes the whole plan first, then follows it. Tree of Thoughts explores several possible paths before picking one.

cards.title: Three Travelers
cards.body: ReAct walks and checks the map at every corner. Plan-and-Execute writes the full route at home, then drives it. Tree of Thoughts imagines three routes and picks the best before leaving.

cards.title: Same Puzzle, Different Moves
cards.body: On a logic puzzle, ReAct guesses, checks, and adjusts. Plan-and-Execute lists all deductions upfront. Tree of Thoughts branches out, tries a few guesses in parallel, and keeps the winner.

cards.title: Not 'One Best Planner'
cards.body: Myth: one planner always wins. Truth: it depends on the puzzle. Simple tasks favor ReAct; multi-path puzzles favor Tree of Thoughts.

cards.title: Step-by-Step vs Explore-First
cards.body: Some planners commit to one path; others explore many first.

left.text: One step at a time, adjusting as it goes

right.text: Branches into many paths, then picks the best

exercises.prompt: A puzzle has many possible paths and dead ends. Which planner is best suited to explore several options before committing?
exercises.explain: Tree of Thoughts branches into multiple possible paths and compares them, making it strong on puzzles with many options and dead ends.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Plan-and-Execute writes the whole plan first, then carries it out.
exercises.explain: That's exactly its style: it drafts the full plan upfront, then executes the steps in order, unlike ReAct which decides one step at a time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about ReAct's style.
exercises.sentence: ReAct thinks and acts one step at a time, adjusting after each _____.
exercises.explain: ReAct interleaves reasoning and action, reacting to what happens after every step before choosing the next one.

exercises.prompt: Which description of comparing planners sounds AI-generated (overly generic and vague)?

options.text: Planners can be leveraged synergistically to optimize holistic solution paradigms across all problem spaces.
On a maze, ReAct backtracked twice, while Tree of Thoughts tried three routes at once.
Plan-and-Execute finished fastest on the simple sorting task because it didn't second-guess.

exercises.explain: The first is vague buzzword filler with no concrete detail, a common sign of low-effort AI text. The others give specific, checkable behavior.

exercises.prompt: You give all three planners the SAME tricky puzzle with several dead-end paths. ReAct keeps hitting dead ends and backtracking, Plan-and-Execute's upfront plan breaks when reality differs, and Tree of Thoughts explores three branches and keeps the best. What's the key lesson?

options.text: The best planner depends on the puzzle: multi-path puzzles favor exploring options first, like Tree of Thoughts
ReAct is always the worst planner no matter the task
Planning never helps; agents should guess randomly
Plan-and-Execute wins every puzzle because it plans everything upfront

exercises.explain: No single planner wins everywhere. On puzzles full of dead ends, exploring several branches first (Tree of Thoughts) shines, while ReAct fits simple adaptive tasks and Plan-and-Execute fits predictable ones. Match the planner to the puzzle.
